name: The Molecular Bond That Helps Secure Your Memories | Quanta Magazine
abstract: Neuroscientists Todd Sacktor and André Fenton have identified a molecular mechanism that may explain how memories persist for decades despite the constant turnover of brain molecules. Their research, published in Science Advances, reveals that a stable, persistent bond between two proteins, PKMζ and KIBRA, is crucial for strengthening and maintaining synaptic connections, which are fundamental to memory formation. This protein complex ensures that as individual proteins degrade, new ones can replace them while preserving the integrity and location of the bond, thereby perpetuating the memory trace. This discovery offers a compelling answer to Francis Crick's long-standing dilemma regarding the longevity of memories versus the ephemeral nature of biological molecules, suggesting that the interaction, rather than individual components, stores the memory.
